blurb: The ladyfinger cactus in its crested (cristate) form — instead of neat fingers, the growing point fans into undulating, brain-like folds packed with soft golden spines. A living squiggle.

care.light: Bright light with plenty of direct sun keeps it tight and well-spined; too little light and the crest grows soft and pale. Give a little shade only in extreme summer heat.
care.water: Soak-and-dry and on the sparing side — water thoroughly, then wait until bone dry. Keep it nearly dry through winter.
care.soil: A gritty cactus mix that drains almost instantly.
care.temperature: Room temperature; keep above ~35°F. A cool, dry winter rest encourages spring flowers.
care.dormancy: A summer grower that rests cool and dry in winter — that dry rest is what triggers the ring of flowers.
care.feeding: A dilute low-nitrogen cactus feed once or twice in summer.
care.potting: Repot infrequently; the crest can hold water in its folds, so keep the mix low around the base and never let water sit in the crown.
care.propagation: By cutting and rooting sections of the crest (fiddly) or from any normal offsets it throws.
care.bloom: A crown of small cream-to-yellow flowers, often in a ring, after a cool dry winter.
care.troubles: Rot from water trapped in the crest, plus mealybugs and red spider mites.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic — but the fine spines catch skin, so handle with care.
